words.shortDefinition: Agile framework for iterative development and sustaining complex products.
words.simpleDefinition: Iterative framework for delivering work in short, fixed cycles called sprints — includes defined roles like Scrum Master and Product Owner. Enables teams to inspect progress and adapt quickly to changing requirements.
words.longDefinition: Scrum focuses on incremental delivery through time-boxed iterations. It emphasizes three pillars: transparency, inspection, and adaptation. Teams operate with specialized roles (Product Owner, Scrum Master, Developers) and follow specific ceremonies including Sprint Planning, Daily Stand-ups, Sprint Reviews, and Retrospectives to ensure continuous improvement and alignment with business value.
words.connector: Think of Scrum like a relay race — broken into fixed legs with handoff checks to ensure the team stays on pace.
words.techContext: Our engineering team uses Scrum to deliver new software increments every two weeks.
words.exampleSentence: The engineering team adopted Scrum to deliver shippable software increments every two weeks, improving stakeholder transparency.
words.etymology: from the game of rugby, where a 'scrum' is a way of restarting play involving players packing closely together.

words.shortDefinition: Visual system for managing work as it flows through a process.
words.simpleDefinition: Visual agile method managing work as cards moving through board columns — tracks flow from backlog to completion. Uses strict Work in Progress (WIP) limits to identify bottlenecks and ensure continuous delivery.
words.longDefinition: Kanban prioritizes flow and continuous delivery rather than fixed-length iterations. It visualizes the entire value stream on a Kanban board, allowing teams to see work-in-progress and potential impediments. By limiting WIP, teams reduce context switching and improve lead times, fostering a culture of continuous improvement through evolutionary change rather than disruptive shifts.
words.connector: Think of Kanban like a sushi conveyor belt — only a limited number of dishes move at once, advancing steadily.
words.techContext: Our support team uses a Kanban board to manage incoming bug reports and ensure they are addressed in order of priority.
words.exampleSentence: Switching to Kanban allowed the support team to manage incoming bug reports more effectively by visualizing the entire support queue.

words.shortDefinition: Relative unit of measure expressing the overall effort required for a story.
words.simpleDefinition: Abstract units used to estimate task complexity and relative effort — not based on hours, but a shared scale reflecting difficulty. Helps teams focus on the size of the work rather than precise time estimates.
words.longDefinition: Story points allow teams to estimate work based on complexity, risk, and effort relative to previous tasks. Most teams use the Fibonacci sequence (1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13) to reflect the increasing uncertainty as tasks grow larger. This approach helps stabilize velocity over time by removing individual variations in developer speed from the initial sizing.
words.connector: Think of story points like T-shirt sizes — S, M, L — a shared language for size without pretending to know exact measurements.
words.techContext: We assigned 5 story points to the new authentication feature because of the complexity involved in supporting multi-factor auth.
words.exampleSentence: Estimating in story points helps our team focus on the relative complexity of features rather than debating exact clock hours.

words.shortDefinition: Average amount of work a team completes during a single sprint.
words.simpleDefinition: Agile metric representing the average story points a team finishes per sprint — used for long-term forecasting. Helps stakeholders predict when specific roadmap features will likely be completed based on historical team performance.
words.longDefinition: Velocity is calculated by summing the story points of all 'Done' user stories at the end of a sprint. While it varies between teams and should never be used as a productivity comparison, it provides an internal baseline for capacity planning. Consistent velocity allows product owners to make data-driven decisions about release dates and scope adjustments.
words.connector: Think of velocity like a car's average speed — once known, you can reliably estimate your arrival time at a destination.
words.techContext: Our average velocity is 45 points per sprint, which helps our Product Owner plan our roadmap for the next quarter.
words.exampleSentence: The team's velocity dropped this week because several developers were attending a security conference, reducing our total sprint capacity.

words.shortDefinition: Graphical representation of remaining work versus time in a sprint.
words.simpleDefinition: Visual graph tracking work left against time — shows if the team is on pace to complete the sprint backlog. Serves as an early warning system for scope creep or potential timing issues.
words.longDefinition: A burndown chart plots the remaining effort (usually in story points or tasks) on the Y-axis against the days remaining in the iteration on the X-axis. An ideal trend line provides a baseline for comparison. Steep drops indicate completion of major tasks, while flat lines suggest blockers or underestimated complexity that need immediate attention.
words.connector: Think of a burndown chart like a fuel gauge — it shows whether you're consuming resources at the right rate to reach your goal.
words.techContext: Looking at the burndown chart, we realized we were lagging behind and decided to move one low-priority task back to the backlog.
words.exampleSentence: The burndown chart showed a significant plateau mid-week, prompting the team to discuss a major infrastructure blocker during the stand-up.

words.shortDefinition: Time-boxed iteration where specific work must be completed and reviewed.
words.simpleDefinition: Fixed-length development cycle — usually one to four weeks — where the team commits to a specific goal. Each cycle results in a potentially shippable product increment that satisfies the team's definition of done.
words.longDefinition: Sprints provide the cadence for Scrum teams, creating a steady rhythm of delivery and feedback. Once a sprint begins, its duration is locked to maintain predictability. The goal of a sprint is to transform selected backlog items into a functional increment, allowing for frequent pivots based on stakeholder feedback and market changes.
words.connector: Think of a sprint like a TV production week — a fixed deadline, a clear episode deliverable, and a reset for the next.
words.techContext: We are currently in the middle of Sprint 24, focusing on improving the mobile app's performance.
words.exampleSentence: Our two-week sprint cycle ensures that we never go more than fourteen days without showing new progress to our stakeholders.
words.etymology: from Old Norse spretta ('to jump up'), used in athletics for short-distance races.

words.shortDefinition: Ordered list of every known requirement needed in the product.
words.simpleDefinition: Master prioritized list of all features, fixes, and improvements needed — managed by the product owner. Acts as the single source of truth for every change the team might eventually implement during the product lifecycle.
words.longDefinition: The product backlog is a dynamic, living document that evolves as the product grows and market conditions shift. Items at the top are more granular and ready for development, while lower items are broader and less defined. It is continuously refined to ensure the team always works on the highest-value initiatives first.
words.connector: Think of the product backlog like a restaurant's waiting list — names are added, priorities shift, and you always seat the top party first.
words.techContext: I spent the afternoon cleaning up the product backlog and closing old feature requests that are no longer relevant.
words.exampleSentence: The product owner moved the security patch to the top of the product backlog following the latest vulnerability report.

words.shortDefinition: Short daily synchronization meeting to plan the next 24 hours.
words.simpleDefinition: Brief daily sync where team members coordinate activities and surface blockers — focuses on progress since yesterday and plans for today. Keeps the entire team aligned and ensures obstacles are identified and resolved immediately.
words.longDefinition: Usually limited to 15 minutes, the daily stand-up is not a status report for management but a planning session for the developers. By answering what was done, what will be done, and what is blocked, the team identifies opportunities for collaboration and ensures the sprint goal remains achievable throughout the iteration.
words.connector: Think of a stand-up like a morning huddle in a kitchen — brief, focused on immediate orders, and identifying missing ingredients.
words.techContext: At the daily stand-up, I mentioned that I'm blocked by a delay in the API documentation from the other team.
words.exampleSentence: We used the daily stand-up to reassign tasks after realizing the database migration was more complex than initially estimated.
words.etymology: Descriptive of the meeting's daily frequency and standing posture.

words.shortDefinition: End-of-sprint meeting to discuss process improvements and team dynamics.
words.simpleDefinition: Reflective session at each sprint's conclusion — where the team analyzes processes, tools, and collaboration. Goal is identifying actionable improvements to implement in the next iteration, fostering a culture of continuous process optimization.
words.longDefinition: The retrospective is the primary vehicle for 'adaptation' in Scrum. The team looks inward to identify what went well, what failed, and why. By committing to one or two specific process changes per sprint, the team ensures they are constantly evolving their workflow to become more efficient and cohesive over time.
words.connector: Think of a retrospective like a post-game film review — analyzing tactics to improve performance before the next match starts.
words.techContext: In our last retrospective, we decided to try using a new automated testing tool to reduce manual QA time.
words.exampleSentence: During the retrospective, the team decided to introduce mandatory peer reviews for all database schema changes to prevent production errors.

words.shortDefinition: Informal description of a feature written from the end-user perspective.
words.simpleDefinition: Plain-language feature description focusing on user value — using the 'As a [role], I want [goal] so that [benefit]' format. Ensures developers build for real human needs rather than just following rigid technical specifications.
words.longDefinition: User stories shift the focus from writing about requirements to talking about them. They are intended to be small enough to fit within a sprint and serve as a placeholder for a conversation between the team and the product owner, ensuring that the final implementation actually solves a user problem.
words.connector: Think of a user story like a customer's request at a deli — 'As a hungry worker, I want a quick sandwich so I can eat before my meeting.'
words.techContext: I added a user story for the search feature so the developers understand why the 'sort by price' filter is important for our customers.
words.exampleSentence: The team refined the user story to ensure it met the INVEST criteria: Independent, Negotiable, Valuable, Estimable, Small, and Testable.

codeExample.code:
As a premium subscriber,
I want to download videos for offline viewing,
So that I can watch content without an internet connection.

words.shortDefinition: Conditions a software product must satisfy to be accepted.
words.simpleDefinition: Detailed conditions a user story must meet to be considered complete — providing a binary pass/fail checklist. Ensures stakeholders and the development team share a clear, unambiguous definition of what success looks like.
words.longDefinition: Acceptance criteria define the boundaries of a user story and guide development and testing. They reduce ambiguity by specifying edge cases, UI requirements, and functional constraints. A story is only 'Done' when every criterion is verified, preventing 'scope creep' and ensuring the delivered feature meets business expectations.
words.connector: Think of acceptance criteria like a pilot's pre-flight checklist — every item must be checked before the plane is cleared for takeoff.
words.techContext: The user story is almost finished, but I still need to satisfy the acceptance criteria regarding error handling for invalid credit cards.
words.exampleSentence: We added an acceptance criterion specifying that the login page must load in under two seconds on 3G connections.
words.etymology: from Latin acceptare ('to take, receive') + criteria ('standards').

words.shortDefinition: Shared understanding of the quality requirements for every completed item.
words.simpleDefinition: Agreed-upon checklist all work must pass before it's considered finished — usually including code review, testing, and documentation. Prevents partially-completed work from being labeled as 'done' and accumulating hidden technical debt.
words.longDefinition: The Definition of Done (DoD) is a formal description of the state of the increment when it meets the quality measures required for the product. While acceptance criteria are specific to a story, the DoD applies to all stories, ensuring consistent quality, security, and maintainability across the entire software system.
words.connector: Think of the definition of done like a health inspector's audit — the restaurant isn't 'done' cleaning until every surface meets the standard.
words.techContext: According to our Definition of Done, the feature isn't finished until the security scan has come back clean.
words.exampleSentence: Our Definition of Done now requires that all new features include automated integration tests and updated API documentation.

words.shortDefinition: Large body of work later decomposed into smaller user stories.
words.simpleDefinition: Significant body of work too large for one sprint — broken into multiple smaller stories to be delivered iteratively. Allows high-level tracking of major product initiatives while keeping daily development tasks manageable.
words.longDefinition: Epics represent large-scale features or initiatives that may span several sprints or even months. They provide a way to organize the backlog into logical themes. By breaking epics down into smaller, actionable user stories, teams can deliver value incrementally rather than waiting for the entire epic to be finished.
words.connector: Think of an epic like a novel — it's too big to read at once, so you tackle it one chapter at a time.
words.techContext: We created an epic for the 'Mobile App Redesign' which contains over twenty individual user stories.
words.exampleSentence: The 'User Authentication' epic was broken down into several stories, including social login, password reset, and multi-factor auth support.
words.etymology: from Greek epikos, traditionally referring to a long poem about heroic deeds.

words.shortDefinition: Servant-leader responsible for facilitating and coaching the Scrum process.
words.simpleDefinition: Team facilitator responsible for removing blockers and coaching Scrum practices — ensures the team can work without outside interference. Focuses on process health and team self-organization rather than direct project management or task assignment.
words.longDefinition: The Scrum Master is a servant-leader for the Scrum Team. They help those outside the team understand which of their interactions with the team are helpful and which aren't. They facilitate Scrum events, remove impediments, and coach the team on how to manage their own work within the Scrum framework.
words.connector: Think of a Scrum Master like a referee — they don't play the game, but they ensure everyone follows the rules and play continues smoothly.
words.techContext: The Scrum Master facilitated the meeting and helped us resolve a conflict regarding how to estimate the new technical debt tasks.
words.exampleSentence: Our Scrum Master successfully negotiated with the infrastructure team to prioritize our server access requests, unblocking the entire sprint.

words.shortDefinition: Person responsible for maximizing product value and managing the backlog.
words.simpleDefinition: Team member representing stakeholders and users — owns the product backlog and decides work priority. Acts as the definitive voice for 'what' gets built, ensuring the team's effort translates into maximum business and user value.
words.longDefinition: The Product Owner is the sole person responsible for managing the Product Backlog. This includes clearly expressing backlog items, ordering them to best achieve goals, and ensuring the development team understands them. They must balance the needs of multiple stakeholders while making tough decisions about what features to defer or cancel.
words.connector: Think of a product owner like a movie producer — they decide which scenes are worth the budget to get the best final film.
words.techContext: I met with the Product Owner to discuss the priority of the new API features for our enterprise clients.
words.exampleSentence: The Product Owner decided to pivot our strategy after seeing the low engagement metrics from the initial beta release.

words.shortDefinition: Constraint on the maximum items allowed in a workflow state.
words.simpleDefinition: Constraint on work items in a given stage — like the 'In Progress' column. Forces the team to finish current work before starting new tasks, which reduces context switching and highlights process bottlenecks immediately.
words.longDefinition: Work In Progress (WIP) limits are a core component of Kanban. By restricting how much can be handled at once, teams increase 'flow' and reduce 'lead time'. When a limit is hit, the team must collaborate to move existing items to completion before taking on new ones, preventing stalls.
words.connector: Think of a WIP limit like a small kitchen counter — you can only prep three meals at once, or the workspace becomes unusable.
words.techContext: We hit our WIP limit in the 'Testing' column, so two developers stopped coding and started helping the QA team clear the backlog.
words.exampleSentence: By lowering our WIP limit from five to three, we saw a 20% improvement in our feature delivery speed.

words.shortDefinition: Time taken to complete a task from start to finish.
words.simpleDefinition: Measurement of time from when development begins until an item is finished — focuses on the active work phase. Provides a key indicator of internal team efficiency and the predictability of the development process.
words.longDefinition: Cycle time measures the velocity of a single work item through the system. Unlike lead time, it only tracks the period where the team is actively working on the task. Lowering cycle time usually involves reducing friction in the development environment, improving automation, and ensuring smaller, more manageable task sizes.
words.connector: Think of cycle time like a pizza's baking time — from the moment it enters the oven until it's ready to serve.
words.techContext: By automating our deployment process, we reduced our average cycle time for bug fixes from 4 days to 6 hours.
words.exampleSentence: Our average cycle time for CSS changes dropped significantly after we implemented a live-reloading style guide for the designers.

words.shortDefinition: Total time from request creation to fulfillment and delivery.
words.simpleDefinition: Total elapsed time from a customer request to final delivery — includes time spent waiting in the backlog. Measures the responsiveness of the entire organization from the user's perspective, reflecting the true speed of the value stream.
words.longDefinition: Lead time captures the end-to-end customer experience. It includes the 'waiting time' in the product backlog before a developer even begins work. Reducing lead time often requires improving business processes, refining requirements faster, and ensuring the team isn't over-burdened by a massive, stagnant backlog of unrefined ideas.
words.connector: Think of lead time like ordering a custom car — the time from when you sign the contract until it sits in your driveway.
words.techContext: Our customers are complaining that lead time for new features is over six months, so we need to improve our backlog refinement process.
words.exampleSentence: We reduced our lead time by 30% by involving developers earlier in the product discovery phase, allowing them to start technical prep sooner.

words.shortDefinition: Implied cost of rework caused by choosing easy, temporary solutions.
words.simpleDefinition: Implied cost of future rework caused by choosing shortcuts today over sustainable, long-term solutions — accumulates 'interest' as code becomes harder to maintain. Requires regular 'repayment' through refactoring to prevent system stagnation and declining development velocity.
words.longDefinition: Technical debt is a metaphor for the trade-off between speed and quality. While sometimes necessary to hit a deadline, unmanaged debt makes future changes more expensive and error-prone. High levels of debt lead to fragile systems where a small change in one area causes unexpected failures in another, eventually halting all new feature development.
words.connector: Think of technical debt like a credit card — spending now is easy, but the interest makes everything more expensive later.
words.techContext: We decided to take on some technical debt by skipping the unit tests for this prototype, but we've added a task to the backlog to fix it next sprint.
words.exampleSentence: The team spent an entire month-long sprint just paying down technical debt that was causing frequent production outages.
words.etymology: Metaphor comparing software maintenance to financial 'debt'.

words.shortDefinition: Sequential development process where each phase must finish before the next.
words.simpleDefinition: Traditional, linear development approach — requirements, design, implementation, testing — where phases never overlap. Often criticized in software for being too rigid and delaying user feedback until the very end of a long, expensive development cycle.
words.longDefinition: In a waterfall model, progress flows downward through established gates. This works well for predictable manufacturing but often fails in software because requirements usually change once users see the product. The lack of iteration means that errors discovered in the final testing phase can be catastrophic and prohibitively expensive to fix.
words.connector: Think of waterfall like building a bridge — you can't decide to move the supports once the concrete is poured.
words.techContext: We moved away from the Waterfall model because it took us too long to get feedback from real users.
words.exampleSentence: The project failed because it followed a strict Waterfall approach that didn't allow for the major requirement changes requested mid-year.
words.etymology: Metaphor for a process that flows in one direction, like water over a cliff.

words.shortDefinition: Consensus-based gamified technique for estimating task size and complexity.
words.simpleDefinition: Estimation technique where team members reveal story-point bids simultaneously — preventing 'anchor bias' from vocal participants. Discrepancies in bids spark essential discussions about hidden technical complexities or requirement misunderstandings before the work ever begins.
words.longDefinition: During planning poker, participants use cards with Fibonacci numbers to vote on the relative size of a user story. If votes differ significantly, the outliers explain their reasoning. This collaborative process ensures that the entire team understands the technical approach and that estimates reflect the collective wisdom rather than just one person's opinion.
words.connector: Think of planning poker like a silent auction — everyone reveals their bid at once so nobody is influenced by others.
words.techContext: We used Planning Poker this morning to estimate the remaining stories in the backlog, and it helped us uncover some hidden complexities in the UI design.
words.exampleSentence: Planning poker revealed that the senior developer expected the task to be easy, while the junior developer saw major architectural risks.

words.shortDefinition: Business-readable language for defining test cases and behavior.
words.simpleDefinition: Standard syntax for writing acceptance tests in plain-English Given/When/Then format — readable by stakeholders and executable by automated frameworks. Bridges the communication gap between business requirements and technical verification scripts.
words.longDefinition: Gherkin serves as the foundation for Behavior Driven Development (BDD). By describing how a feature should behave in human-readable terms, it allows non-technical product owners to verify that developers are building the right thing. These 'feature files' can then be automatically converted into test scripts by tools like Cucumber or Behave.
words.connector: Think of Gherkin like a recipe — anyone can read it to understand the result, but a machine can follow it precisely.
words.techContext: I wrote a Gherkin script to define the acceptance criteria for the new password reset flow.
words.exampleSentence: The QA engineer converted our Gherkin requirements into automated Selenium tests that run after every code commit.
words.etymology: Named after the 'gherkin' (pickled cucumber), following the Cucumber testing tool theme.

codeExample.code:
Feature: Password Reset
  Scenario: User resets forgotten password
    Given the user is on the login page
    When they click 'Forgot Password' and enter their email
    Then they should receive a reset link via email

words.shortDefinition: Subset of product backlog items selected for the current sprint.
words.simpleDefinition: Highly-focused list of items the team commits to delivering during the current iteration — includes a concrete plan for achieving the sprint goal. Owned exclusively by the developers, it evolves as the team discovers more about the work.
words.longDefinition: The sprint backlog is the set of items selected from the product backlog for the sprint, plus a plan for delivering the product increment and realizing the sprint goal. It provides a highly visible, real-time picture of the work that the team plans to accomplish, making the progress and blockers transparent to everyone involved.
words.connector: Think of a sprint backlog like a tray at a buffet — you've selected exactly what you can finish in one sitting.
words.techContext: I moved the API integration task into the Sprint Backlog since we've already started working on it.
words.exampleSentence: The team realized they couldn't finish everything in the Sprint Backlog, so they worked with the Product Owner to negotiate the scope.

words.shortDefinition: Cumulative Flow Diagram; chart showing work item status over time.
words.simpleDefinition: Visual chart plotting work items across different workflow stages over time — revealing process stability and flow. Horizontal bands indicate time spent in stages, while vertical bands show the quantity of items, helping identify systemic bottlenecks.
words.longDefinition: A CFD provides a comprehensive view of the health of a Kanban system. By looking at the slope of the 'Done' line compared to the 'To Do' line, teams can see if their delivery rate is keeping pace with demand. Bulges in specific bands indicate where work is piling up, allowing teams to adjust WIP limits or reassign resources.
words.connector: Think of a CFD like a cross-section of a river — you can see where the water is pooling and where it flows freely.
words.techContext: The CFD showed that the 'Review' stage was growing wider, indicating that our code reviews are taking longer than they used to.
words.exampleSentence: Our CFD confirmed that our workflow is stable, with the distance between the 'In Progress' and 'Done' lines remaining consistent for months.

words.shortDefinition: Ongoing process of adding detail and estimates to backlog items.
words.simpleDefinition: Continuous process of reviewing, detailing, and estimating upcoming backlog items — ensures user stories are 'ready' for development. Prevents sprint planning from becoming a marathon of requirement discovery by resolving ambiguities and splitting large items early.
words.longDefinition: Backlog refinement (or grooming) is an ongoing activity where the Product Owner and the Developers collaborate to add detail, estimates, and order to items in the Product Backlog. This ensures that the team has a clear understanding of upcoming work, reducing the risk of starting a sprint with vague or impossible requirements.
words.connector: Think of refinement like kitchen prep — doing the chopping and measuring before service so the chef can cook at full speed.
words.techContext: We spent an hour in backlog refinement to break down the large search epic into five smaller, manageable user stories.
words.exampleSentence: Regular refinement sessions have made our planning meetings much more efficient, as we no longer have to debate basic requirements mid-sprint.
